intro:
The obedience of Christ is the perfect, whole-life submission of the incarnate Son to the will of His Father — His glad, unbroken conformity to the law and purpose of God from the manger to the cross. Reformed theology has long distinguished two aspects, though they are never separated: His active obedience, by which He positively fulfilled all the righteous requirements of God’s law on behalf of His people, and His passive obedience, by which He submitted to suffering and death in their place. As the last Adam and covenant Mediator, Christ rendered the obedience that the first Adam and all his children failed to give.

This obedience is the deep structure of the gospel. Where Adam’s disobedience brought condemnation, Christ’s obedience brings life: “For as by the one man’s disobedience the many were made sinners, so by the one man’s obedience the many will be made righteous” (Romans 5:19). The Son came into the world with this very purpose on His lips: “Behold, I have come to do your will, O God” (Hebrews 10:7; cf. Psalm 40:7–8), and “I have come down from heaven, not to do my own will but the will of him who sent me” (John 6:38).

Scripture traces this obedience across His whole life. He was “born under the law” (Galatians 4:4) and was subject to His parents (Luke 2:51); He insisted on baptism “to fulfill all righteousness” (Matthew 3:15); He could say, “My food is to do the will of him who sent me” (John 4:34). In Gethsemane His obedience was tested to its depths, and He yielded: “Not my will, but yours, be done” (Luke 22:42). “Although he was a son, he learned obedience through what he suffered” (Hebrews 5:8) — not learning to obey where He had been disobedient, but tasting in His flesh the full cost of obedience. It reached its summit at Calvary, where He became “obedient to the point of death, even death on a cross” (Philippians 2:8).

The obedience of Christ is therefore no mere example to admire; it is the very righteousness in which sinners stand. Because He kept the covenant we broke and bore the penalty we owed, His obedience is reckoned to all who are united to Him by faith, so that they are “made righteous” before God (Romans 5:19; 2 Corinthians 5:21). His perfect obedience also becomes the ground and pattern of theirs: redeemed as sons, they too learn obedience, “not as a slave but… as a beloved child” (cf. Romans 6:17; Hebrews 5:9). In the obedient Son, the broken law is honored, the guilty are justified, and the disobedient are remade into a people who delight to do the will of God.
